coredata.dc:description: Multimode sensors are required to enable detect of hazardous substances in different media. A sensing platform based on metal organic frameworks (MOF) and its derivatives was established to detect triethylamine (TEA) gas and TEA aqueous solution in the range of 1–100 ppm. The carbon dots (CDs) were selected as the light-energy conversion module to improve greatly the photoelectric performance of sensors. On the one hand, the dual-emission ratio type fluorescent sensor was built by CDs and MOF. The CDs@ZIF-8(In) showed excellent fluorescence emission enhancement at 440 nm and 610 nm in a dose-dependent manner when TEA was added. The change of fluorescence emission intensity of CDs@ZIF-8(In) sensor for TEA in water was at least 3 times than that of other volatile organic compounds, and its detection limit reached 1 ppm. On the other hand, the gas sensor based on MOF derivatives modified by CDs (CDs@DZIF-8(In)) displayed 3.5 times signal response than pure DZIF-8(In), reaching 374.6 to 100 ppm TEA. Moreover, the optimal operating temperature of CDs@DZIF-8(In) sensor was 40 ℃ lower than that of DZIF-8(In) sensor. The excellent fluorescence and electron transfer properties of CDs may play a key role in the design and modification of various types of sensors.

The structure analysis and schematic illustration of the sensing mechanism of CDs@DZIF-8(In).

head.ce:title: Superior triethylamine sensing platform based on MOF activated by carbon dots for photoelectric dual-mode in biphasic system

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multimode sensors are required to enable detect of hazardous substances in different media. A sensing platform based on metal organic frameworks (MOF) and its derivatives was established to detect triethylamine (TEA) gas and TEA aqueous solution in the range of 1–100 ppm. The carbon dots (CDs) were selected as the light-energy conversion module to improve greatly the photoelectric performance of sensors. On the one hand, the dual-emission ratio type fluorescent sensor was built by CDs and MOF. The CDs@ZIF-8(In) showed excellent fluorescence emission enhancement at 440 nm and 610 nm in a dose-dependent manner when TEA was added. The change of fluorescence emission intensity of CDs@ZIF-8(In) sensor for TEA in water was at least 3 times than that of other volatile organic compounds, and its detection limit reached 1 ppm. On the other hand, the gas sensor based on MOF derivatives modified by CDs (CDs@DZIF-8(In)) displayed 3.5 times signal response than pure DZIF-8(In), reaching 374.6 to 100 ppm TEA. Moreover, the optimal operating temperature of CDs@DZIF-8(In) sensor was 40 ℃ lower than that of DZIF-8(In) sensor. The excellent fluorescence and electron transfer properties of CDs may play a key role in the design and modification of various types of sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Triethylamine (TEA) is an important chemical raw material, which is often used in organic synthesis as catalyst, antiseptic, curing agent and solvent. [1–2] However, the TEA gas has strong stimulation and corrosion effects on human respiratory system. Moreover, TEA is slightly soluble in water, and its wastewater is toxic and difficult to degrade, which is very harmful to the ecological environment and human health. [3–4] Therefore, it is urgent to develop a strategy for rapid and ultra-sensitive detection of TEA in different media.

The metal oxide semiconductor, with excellent physical and chemical properties, diverse structures and good stability, can produce strong resistance changes by surface regulation. It is often used as a gas sensitive material for the detection of volatile organic compounds (VOCs), with high sensitivity and fast response and recovery speed. [5–6,45] There are various methods can be used to improve the gas sensing performance of metal oxides, such as, heterojunction construction, surface modification, morphology regulation, heteroatom doping, and so on. [7–10,46] For example, Cheng et al. prepared the quasi-2D rGO-SnS2 hybrids for detecting NO2 gas. The rGO in hybrids can decrease the optimal working temperature from 200 ℃ to 150 ℃ and enhanced almost an order of magnitude response signal when 0.8% rGO was added. [11] Then they also reported that the hierarchical litchi-like In2O3 nanospheres decorated by carbon dots (CDs) for NO2 detection. The response signal of In2O3/CDs is 4 times as high as the pristine In2O3 at working temperature of 50 ℃. The reason can be attributed to the heterjunctions constructed across the interface between the In2O3 and CDs. [12] Ma et al. reported a 100 ppm fish-mesh SnSe nanofibers prepared by uniaxial electrospinning. The SnSe gas sensor showed excellent selectivity and high sensitivity for 10 ppm TEA at 160 ℃, and a low detection base limit (500 ppb). [47] In recent years, the metal organic frameworks (MOFs) is often used as a template to derive metal oxides with more abundant morphology and larger specific surface area. [13–16] For example, it was reported that the ZIF-8 generated porous ZnO nanocube with abundant active sites and connected pore channels after pyrolysis at 500 ℃. It showed excellent sensitivity (Rg/Ra = 51.41) and selectivity to NO2 at the optimal working temperature of 200 ℃. [17] And the derivatives of MOFs, NiO-doped CuO composites (CN-L) displayed a strong response (106%) to 50 ppb H2S at 180 ℃. What’s more, the CN-L still has a ppm-level response to H2S at amibient temperature of 4 ℃ and −18 ℃. [18] It indicated that the feasibility of detecting meat spoilage under low temperature storage. Surprisingly, MOFs often have excellent fluorescence properties in addition to being used as templates. [19–21] Moreover, the MOF-based fluorescent materials operate in liquid phase systems usually and are often used to detect heavy metal ions, organic solvents and so on. [48] For example, the NH2-UiO-66 modified with both Au nanoparticles and thiol-functionalized CDs (HS-C/Au(x)/UiO-66) shows outstanding sensitivity to Pb2+. Among them, the HS-C/Au(1.4)/UiO-66 exhibited a quenching response with the limit of detection of 0.08 ppb and five cycle. [22] Furthermore, the ratiometric fluorescent sensors have more advantage in the analysis of multiple targets. The MOF based triemissive composite was constructed to used as a viable strategy for detecting disinfectants with μM-level responses. Specifically, the blue emissive NDC-SO3Na, green emissive BDC-diOH and red emissive MB were incorporated into UiO-66 framework. [23] Especially, due to flexible surface state, abundant functional groups, ultra-small size, and so on, CDs are often used as a light energy conversion component to construct ratiometric fluorescent sensors with bulk materials. [26–30] For example, Xu et al. designed a ratiometric and colorimetric fluorescent probe for Hg2+ based on dual-emissive MOF hybrids with CDs and Eu3+. [24] And Fu et al. constructed a dual-emission MOF equipped with CDs for detection of doxycycline and MnO4 -.[25].

Here, sensing platform was expected to build. It can realize the dual ultra-sensitive detection of TEA in air and water through a variety of detection methods. Specifically, the Indium exchanged interpenetrating twin ZIF-8 (ZIF-8(In)), as the main material, provides rich active sites and blue fluorescence, and CDs, as the surface modifier, prepared by one-step hydrothermal method provide red fluorescence. The ratio type fluorescent probe CDs@ZIF-8(In) was constructed to detect trace amounts of TEA in water. The excellent anti-interference is given to CDs@ZIF-8(In) due to two strong and independent fluorescence emission peaks, and visual detection based on CDs@ZIF-8(In) is expected to be developed due to the fluorescence enhanced detection. Subsequently, the annealed ZIF-8 derivatives (DZIF-8(In)) were used as bulk materials and CDs as surface modifiers to construct the gas sensor to detect TEA gas in the air. The sensing platform composed of the two detection methods has super sensitive response and excellent selectivity to TEA in water and air. The structure and morphology of CDs@ZIF-8(In) and CDs@DZIF-8(In) were characterized, and the sensing mechanisms were analyzed.

The precursor materials include, Zinc acetate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O, 99.99%, Aladdin, Shanghai, China), 2-Methylimidazole (C4H6N2, 98.0%, Aladdin, Shanghai, China), Hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium Bromide (C19H42BrN, CTAB, 99%, Aladdin, Shanghai, China), Indium acetate (In(CH3COO)3·4H2O, 99.99%, Aladdin, Shanghai, China), urea (H2NCONH2, 99.5%, Aladdin, Shanghai, China), P-phenylenediamine (C6H8N2, 99.0%, Aladdin, Shanghai, China), ethanol (CH3CH2OH, 95%, Aladdin, Shanghai, China), glycol (C2H6O2, 99.8%, Aladdin, Shanghai, China), Nasodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99.99%, Aladdin, Shanghai, China), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 98%, Aladdin, Shanghai, China), copper chloride (CuCl2·2H2O, 99.9%, Aladdin, Shanghai, China), lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2, ≥99.99%, Aladdin, Shanghai, China), cadmium chloride (CdCl2·2.5H2O, 98%, Aladdin, Shanghai, China) and distilled water. All chemical reagents involved in the experiments are of analytical purity and used directly without further purification.

The CDs was optimized from our previous work. [49] 2.40 g of urea and 3.00 g of p-phenylenediamine were dissolved in 20 mL of ethanol water solution. Subsequently, the solvothermal reaction was carried out with 200 ℃ for 3 h. The dark brown solution was purified to obtain pure CDs solution.

The Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O (0.22 g) and CTAB (0.12 g) were added in 32 mL deionized water and stirred to form solvent A. The 2-Methylimidazole (2.46 g) was added in 32 mL deionized water to form solvent B. The solution A was added to solution B and stirred evenly to form solution C. Then solution C was transferred to a Teflon-sealed autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 24 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the resultant milky product was washed three times with methanol and collected by centrifuging at 10,000 rpm for 5 min, drying in an air oven at 60 ℃ overnight. The ion exchange process is as follows: 0.10 g of In(CH3COO)3·4H2O was dissolved in a mixed solvent of 1:3 ethylene glycol and ethanol and stirred for 40 min. Then 0.15 g interpenetrating twin ZIF-8 was added and sonicated for 30 min to form a homogeneous solution. Finally, the hydrothermal reaction was carried out at 160 ℃ for 4 h. The obtained sample was washed with ethanol and deionized water several times to finally obtain pure ZIF-8(In).

1, 2, 3 mL of 1.5 mg/L CDs water ethanol solutions were added to 100 mL of 3 mg/L ZIF-8(In) aqueous solution, respectively. Then the mixed solution was heated and stirred at 60 ℃ for 5 h to obtain a series of CDs@ZIF-8(In), which are denoted as 1-CDs@ZIF-8(In), 2-CDs@ZIF-8(In) and 3-CDs@ZIF-8(In), respectively.

A TF20 transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI, United States) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi Regulus8100) were used to observed the morphology of CDs, ZIF-8(In), DZIF-8(In) and CDs@DZIF-8(In). The adsorption–desorption isotherms of nitrogen measurement were acted on an automated surface analyzer (Quantachrome, Quadrasord SI, USA). The phase composition and the crystalline structure of the above-prepared samples were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, D/MAX2200V PC, Cu Ka1, k = 1.5406 A°, Rigaku, Japan), with the scanning range from 5 to 80°. The fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was recorded on a Nicolet Nexus 670 FTIR Spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a Kα spectrometer (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) with an Al Kα X-ray source. The UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Hitachi UH-3900 spectrophotometer and the photo-luminescence (PL) spectra were obtained on a FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and measurement of gas sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication method of the sensor is based on the previous literature. [32–33].

The performance of gas sensor was tested by a WS-30A gas sensor measurement system (TS64, Zhengzhou Winsen Electronic Technology Co., Ltd). The gas response value is defined as S = Ra/Rg, where Ra is the resistance of the gas sensor in air, and Rg is the resistance of the gas sensor in the gas under test. Response and recovery time is the time that response and recovery of sensor reaches 90% of total resistance change when detecting target gas.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure of the CDs, ZIF-8(In), CDs@ZIF-8(In), DZIF-8(In) and CDs@DZIF-8(In)
ce:section.ce:para: The clear and high-quality SEM and TEM images were obtained to evaluate the morphology of samples. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the interpenetrating twin ZIF-8 with complete particle size and good monodispersity is about 1–2 µm. After ion exchange with indium, the morphology of ZIF-8 (In) has not changed compared with ZIF-8, indicating that the ion exchange process did not cause the collapse of the crystal structure (Fig. 1b). Then ZIF-8(In) and CDs are simply heated and compounded to obtain CDs@ZIF-8(In), as shown in Fig. 1c. As shown in Fig. S8(a), the CDs@ZIF-8(In) with complete particle size and good monodispersity is about 1.8 µm. A large number of CDs are loaded at the surface of ZIF-8(In) (in Fig. S8(b)). Due to the weak crystallinity of CDs and the decrease in photographic contrast caused by ZIF-8(In), it is difficult to observe clear lattice stripes in the black CDs aggregation area (in Fig. S8(c)). The skeleton of DZIF-8(In) obtained by calcining ZIF-8 (In) is complete without collapse, with a diameter of about 1.5 μm (Fig. 1(d-f)). It can be seen that DZIF-8(In) with rich pore structure are assembled and connected by many nanosheets as the smallest structural units (Fig. S1). There are two kinds of nanosheets. One lattice stripe is 0.247 nm, corresponding to the (101) crystal plane of ZnO. The other lattice stripe is 0.292 nm, corresponding to the (222) crystal plane of In2O3. [31] The CDs have good monodispersity and certain crystallinity, with a diameter of about 3–8 nm (Fig. 1g) and a height of about 1–2 nm (Fig. 1h and Fig. S7). The CDs are so small and well dispersed that it is easy to be compounded with ZIF-8 and DZIF-8(In). The skeleton of CDs@DZIF-8(In) with abundant pores has a diameter of about 1.2 μm (Fig. S9(a-c)). As shown in Fig. 1i, the CDs can be clearly observed on DZIF-8(In) nanosheets. The mapping images of CDs@DZIF-8(In) shown the uniform distribution of Zn, In, C, and O elements, indicating that CDs have successfully complexed with DZIF-8(In) (Fig. S9(d-i)).

The Fig. 2 a exhibited that the DZIF-8(In) is good consistent with the standard JCPDS card of In2O3 phase (PDF#71–2195) and ZnO phase (PDF#71–2195). What’s more, compared with of pure DZIF-8(In), the XRD peaks of CDs@DZIF-8(In) has no significant shift, verifying that CDs do not enter into the crystal structures during the process of functionalization. In addition, the obtained ZIF-8 and ZIF-8(In) in this work are pure phases by comparing with the published ZIF-8 XRD data (Fig. 2b). [34] What’s more, all diffraction peaks of ZIF-8(In) are completely consistent with those of pure ZIF-8, indicating that the encapsulation of a small amount of In does not change the nanostructure of ZIF-8. The characteristic diffraction peaks of ZIF-8(In) can be clearly seen at 2θ = 7.4°, 10.4°, 12.7°, 14.7°, 16.4°, 18.0°, and 22.1°, corresponding to (011), (002), (112), (022), (013), (222), and (114) crystal plane of ZIF-8, respectively. [35] And the XRD curve of CDs@ZIF-8(In) appears bulge at about 25°, which is attributed to the graphite like crystal plane of CDs. The high-resolution XPS spectra of CDs, ZIF-8(In), CDs@ZIF-8(In), DZIF-8(In) and CDs@DZIF-8(In) were obtained to explore their chemical structure. The Fig. S2 displays that there are many functional groups on the surface of CDs, such as, CC (283.6 eV), C-O/C-N (284.7 eV), CN (287.8 eV), –CONH- (288.4 eV). [36] As shown in Fig. 2c, the ZIF-8(In) contain C-C/CC (284.5 eV), C-O-C/C-OH (285.1 eV) and C-N (288.1 eV). The content of C-N functional groups carried by CDs@ZIF-8(In) was greatly increased compared with ZIF-8(In) due to the introduction of CDs. What’s more, the high-resolution O1s XPS spectra of ZIF-8(In) and CDs@ZIF-8(In) also show that the content of C-O-C/C-OH (532.5 eV) of CDs@ZIF-8(In) increased obviously compared with ZIF-8(In) (Fig. S3). The FTIR spectra analysis results also proved that the surface functional groups of CDs@ZIF-8(In) are more abundant than ZIF-8(In) (Fig. 2d). [34] It was most notable that the high-resolution O1s spectra of DZIF-8(In) and CDs@DZIF-8(In) can be marked to two peaks as one peak near 529.6 eV attributed to O2– in lattice (Olattice) and the other around 532.1 eV caused by oxygen-related vacancies (defined as oxygen adsorbed (Oads)) (Fig. 2e). It is well known that Olattice is very stable during gas-sensing reactions, and it does not contribute to the gas-sensing performance, while Oads was reported to make an important contribution to the improvement of gas sensing performance. [37] The Olattice/Oads of DZIF-8(In) is 0.46. With the introduction of CDs, the Olattice/Oads of CDs@DZIF-8(In) increases to 1.14, which means that the content of active groups (O2–(ads), O-(ads) and O2 –(ads)) used to detect TEA on surface of CDs@DZIF-8(In) increases sharply, and the gas sensing response will be greatly enhanced. In short, the introduction of CDs provides more active sites for CDs@ZIF-8(In) and CDs@DZIF-8(In) to detect TEA. In Fig. 2f, the BET results show that the specific surface area of ZIF-8(In) was 568.447 m2/g and the pore size distribution was between 6 and 12 nm. Because the ligand was removed by high-temperature calcination, the DZIF-8(In) retains the complete ZIF-8(In) skeleton, the specific surface area decreases to 163.337 m2/g, and the pore size decreases to 4–12 nm. The surface area and pore size of DZIF-8(In) are both reduced compared to ZIF-8(In). It can be attributed to the release of gases, such as CO, CO2, NxOy, H2O, etc. during the pyrolysis of imidazole. [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL and UV–vis spectroscopy of the CDs, ZIF-8(In), CDs@ZIF-8(In)
ce:section.ce:para:
The PL spectra of CDs, ZIF-8(In) and CDs@ZIF-8(In) have been measured to explored their fluorescence properties. As shown in Fig. 3 (a and d), the fluorescence emission peak range of ZIF-8(In) is 370–600 nm with stable and strong blue fluorescence. Its maximum emission peak is located at 450 nm under 360 nm excitation. The emission peak of CDs covers 535–730 nm showing red fluorescent, and the maximum value of emission peak is at 610 nm under 360 nm excitation (Fig. 3(b and e)). The ratio type fluorescence CDs@ZIF-8(In) with two emission peaks was obtained when the CDs and ZIF-8(In) are combined. One emission peak of CDs@ZIF-8(In) is located near 440 nm from ZIF-8(In), and the other is located at 610 nm from CDs (as shown in Fig. 3(c and f)). When the excitation wavelength gradually increases from 345 nm to 392 nm, the emission peak intensity at 440 nm of CDs@ZIF-8(In) first increases and then decreases, while the emission peak intensity at 610 nm gradually increases. The intensities of two emission peaks are basically the same under 360 nm excitation. Therefore, 360 nm was selected as the optimum excitation wavelength for the fluorescence detection experiment of CDs@ZIF-8(In) to TEA. The fluorescence emission of CDs, ZIF-8(In), and CDs@ZIF-8(In) all have the properties of obvious excitation wavelength independence. It means that there are more excitation wavelengths can be selected for the fluorescence detection. [36] Moreover, the stable and strong fluorescence emission is conducive to fluorescence detection in complex environments and exploration of detection mechanism. [29] In Fig. 3g, the emission peak intensity at 610 nm from CDs significantly increased with the addition of CDs, while the emission peak intensity of ZIF-8(In) at 440 nm gradually decreased, which is attributed to the energy transfer process between CDs and ZIF-8(In). The 3-CDs@ZIF-8(In) was selected as the most suitable sample to be used for fluorescence detection of TEA because the intensity of the two emission peaks matched well. The UV diffuse reflectance spectra of ZIF-8(In) and CDs@ZIF-8(In) are comparatively shown in Fig. 3h. Compared with ZIF-8(In), the UV diffuse reflection spectrum of CDs@ZIF-8(In) shows weaker in the wavelength range of 250–550 nm probably due to strong absorption of CDs. In order to obtain the optical band gap of ZIF-8(In) and CDs@ZIF-8(In), the K/S spectra were calculated from reflectance spectra using the Kubelka-Munk function:
F(R) = K/S = (1-R)2/2R(1)

where K, S, and R are the absorption, scattering coefficient, and reflection, respectively. [38] The K/S spectra of the ZIF-8(In) and CDs@ZIF-8(In) derived with the Kubelka-Munk equation is shown in Fig. 3i. The optical band gap of ZIF-8(In) is calculated as 5.15 eV, while the CDs@ZIF-8(In) is 5.10 eV, indicating that the optical band gap of CDs@ZIF-8(In) can be reduced by CDs effectively.

Owing to two strong and stable fluorescence emission peaks, ratio type fluorescence CDs@ZIF-8(In) has stronger anti-interference and better selectivity in detecting TEA. As shown in Fig. 4 a, with the increase of TEA content in water, the intensity of Peak440nm and Peak610nm both increased significantly while their peak positions remained unchanged, showing a typical dose-dependent fluorescence enhancement. The dependence of the fluorescence emission intensity of Peak440nm and Peak610nm on the content of TEA in water was analyzed by establishing mathematical relationship, respectively (Fig. 4c). Furthermore, the emission peak integral area ratio of Peak440 nm to Peak610 nm is obtained (I440/I610), a new functional relationship between I440/I610 and TEA concentration in water is established, as shown in Fig. 4d. The establishment of above three functional relationships ensures that CDs@ZIF-8(In) have excellent selectivity for TEA. The fluorescence detection limit of CDs@ZIF-8(In) for TEA is 1 ppm, and the Ministry of Health of China has set the limit of TEA in water source to 3 ppm, which means that the detection ability of CDs@ZIF-8(In) with perfect practical value for TEA can meet the detection requirements in production and life. [39] As shown in Fig. 4b and S4, there are 11 species of 100 ppm VOCs (TEA, N-butyl, ammonia, ethanol, isopropanol, ethanediol, acetone, methanol, xylene, benzene, toluene, and methanal) were detected to prove the selectivity of CDs@ZIF-8(In) to TEA. Among them, it can be observed that the intensity of the CDs@ZIF-8(In) two fluorescence emission peaks have the most obvious changes in 100 ppm TEA aqueous solution, which are 34,104 (Peak440nm) and 39,462 (Peak610nm), respectively. The change of fluorescence emission intensity of CDs@ZIF-8(In) at 440 nm and 610 nm in TEA aqueous solution is 5 times (440 nm) and 3 times (610 nm) of that in ammonia aqueous solution, respectively, and much higher than that in other VOCs aqueous solutions. In multi-component systems, the response of CDs@ZIF-8(In) to TEA has also been studied, as shown in Fig. S10(a). In the presence of ammonia and n-butyl, CDs@ZIF-8(In) still displays good selectivity for TEA. Moreover, the effects of pH and ions on the CDs@ZIF-8(In) detection results are shown in Fig. S11. pH can affect the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs@ZIF-8(In). The reason is that the electron transfer during the protonation and deprotonation of functional groups such as carboxyl groups affects the photo-generated electron radiation transition luminescence process of CDs@ZIF-8(In) (Fig. S11(a)). In addition, Fig. S11(b) shows the detection results of CDs@ZIF-8(In) for TEA in the presence of ions. The CDs@ZIF-8(In) exhibits the phenomenon of reduced fluorescence because some active sites of CDs@ZIF-8(In) bind to ions, neutralizing lone pair electrons, leading to inactivation of the active sites and fluorescence quenching. Finally, in deionized water with pH = 7, the stability of CDs@ZIF-8(In) and five cycle experiments were performed, as shown in Fig. S12. The fluorescence emission intensity of CDs@ZIF-8(In) remains basically unchanged during 14 days of continuous testing, indicating that CDs@ZIF-8(In) has good long-term stability (Fig. S12(a-b)). After 5 cycle tests of CDs@ZIF-8(In) for TEA were performed, the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs@ZIF-8(In) has slightly decreased. The reason is that a small amount of CDs@ZIF-8(In) nanoparticles are taken away by water (Fig. S12(c-d)). Table S1 shows the comparison of CDs@ZIF-8(In) and several MOF based fluorescence sensors. The CDs@ZIF-8(In) and several other fluorescent sensors all have a detection limit of ppm for organic compounds. More importantly, the CDs@ZIF-8(In) with dual emission fluorescence peaks shows fluorescence enhanced detection, indicating it has strong anti-interference and visual detection prospects. As shown in Fig. 4e, the mechanism of CDs@ZIF-8(In) fluorescence detection for TEA is described simply. When CDs@ZIF-8(In) are in aqueous solution, photo-generated electrons obtained energy in the ground state transition from HOMO level to LUMO level and become excited photo-generated electrons under 360 nm excitation. However, the photo-generated electrons in the excited state can not exist stably due to their high energy. They will return to the ground state in the form of radiative transition, accompanied by the Stokes shift. While in TEA aqueous solution, the –NH2 bond on TEA and C-N, CO, –CONH-, etc. on CDs@ZIF-8(In) can form a large number of hydrogen bonds. The weak conjugated structure can greatly enhance the degree of electron cloud delocalization and increase the concentration of photo-generated electrons, thus improving significantly the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs@ZIF-8(In). [40] In short, the two emission peaks are independent and the fluorescence emission is stable and strong, indicating that CDs@ZIF-8(In) is a fluorescent sensor with strong anti-interference and good selectivity. Moreover, the fluorescence enhanced detection of CDs@ZIF-8(In) are more conducive to the development of visual inspection and real-time monitoring.

The DZIF-8(In) is obtained when ZIF-8(In) was annealed. The response of DZIF-8(In)-based gas sensors to TEA were studied systematically. As we all know, the operating temperature is an important index to show the gas sensor performance. The sensor with lower working temperature is often more practical, because the lower working temperature can effectively reduce energy consumption and prolong the sensor’s service life. [41] The Fig. 5 a shows the response (Ra/Rg) curve of DZIF-8(In) sensor and CDs@DZIF-8(In) sensor to 100 ppm TEA in the range of 140–300 ℃. The value of Ra/Rg of DZIF-8(In) sensor increases gradually with the increase of working temperature until reaching the maximum response value of 107.7 at 200 ℃. It indicates that, at this working temperature, TEA molecule will obtain enough energy to overcome the potential barrier of activation energy on the surface of DZIF-8(In), promoting effectively the redox reaction between the negative oxygen ions on the material surface and TEA gas, improving the response value of the DZIF-8(In) sensor. Then, when the temperature continues to rise from 200 ℃ to 300 ℃, the Ra/Rg values of DZIF-8(In) sensor gradually decrease. It is due to that the desorption capacity of the active group adsorbed on the surface of DZIF-8(In) exceeds the adsorption capacity, leading to the reduction of the reactive active sites and DZIF-8(In) sensor response value. [42] Similarly, the Ra/Rg values of CDs@DZIF-8(In) sensor also show a trend of first strengthening and then weakening with the temperature change. Its optimal working temperature is 160 ℃, significantly lower than that of DZIF-8(In). What’s more, the Ra/Rg value of CDs@DZIF-8(In) is 2.5 times higher than that of DZIF-8(In), reaching 374.6 at 160 ℃. It is because more active sites will be formed after CDs containing rich defects and functional groups contact with DZIF-8(In), which will effectively reduce the additional energy required for TEA molecules to overcome the surface activation energy barrier of materials, that is, reduce the optimal working temperature and enhance the response value of CDs@DZIF-8(In) sensor. [7] In addition, the effect of relative humidity on the DZIF-8(In) sensor and CDs@DZIF-8(In) sensor to 100 ppm TEA was investigated at the optimal working temperature. As shown in Fig. S5, as the relative humidity increases from 37% to 80%, the response value of sensors decrease gradually. When the relative humidity exceeded 54%, the response value of sensors to TEA all decrease significantly. It is because too much water molecules can combine with the surface oxygen of the material, preventing the material from reacting with TEA gas, and reducing the response value. [43] Therefore, in the subsequent studies, the relative humidity was controlled at about 54% to ensure the efficient and stable operation of sensors. The Fig. 5b shows the transient sensing curve of DZIF-8(In) sensor for 100 ppm TEA at 200 ℃ and 54% relative humidity. The response time and recovery time of DZIF-8(In) sensor are 18 s and 55 s, respectively. Similarly, the response time and recovery time of CDs@DZIF-8(In) sensor measured at 180 ℃ and 54% relative humidity are 16 s and 90 s, respectively (Fig. 5c). The introduction of CDs with super multi active group effectively shorten the response time of CDs@DZIF-8(In) sensor and greatly enhances the response signal. After the TEA gas is introduced, the sensor resistance increases rapidly. When the air environment is restored, the sensor resistance can also return to the initial resistance state, proving the excellent sensitivity of two sensors. More importantly, compared with DZIF-8(In), the resistance variation of CDs@DZIF-8(In) to TEA has enhanced by an order of magnitude. The typical dynamic response and recovery curves of DZIF-8(In) sensor and CDs@DZIF-8(In) sensor at different TEA concentrations (1–100 ppm) are shown in Fig. 5 (d-e). It can be observed that the responses of two sensors steadily increase along with the increasing of TEA concentration. Even at a small TEA concentration (1 ppm), the two sensors still have good responses, demonstrating that they can be used to detect trace TEA. Both the European Commission and the American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists (ACGIH) have recommended an exposure threshold of 1 ppm for TEA in the air. [44] It means that DZIF-8(In) and CDs@DZIF-8(In) sensors have the ability to meet the detection needs in production and life, and have good practical value. The function relationships between the response value of the two sensors and TEA concentration are reflected in Fig. S6, respectively. It can be observed that the sensitivity response increases with increasing concentrations of TEA. The good fitting results indicate that two sensors can detect TEA gas in a wide concentration range and potentially for quantitative detection of TEA. The response repeatability is also a key parameter of gas sensor, reflecting the gas sensor stability. In Fig. 5 (f-g), 6 cycles tests of DZIF-8(In) and CDs@DZIF-8(In) sensors were performed continuously at 100 ppm TEA concentration. It can be observed that the dynamic response and recovery curves of two sensors is so stable and repeatable that the resistance can return to the initial state in 6 cycles, suggesting the stable performance and good practicability of sensors. Further, the long-term stability can reflect the durability of the gas sensor. The DZIF-8(In) sensor and CDs@DZIF-8(In) sensor were placed in the air for 15 days. During this period, the response of the two sensors to 100 ppm TEA at the optimal operating temperature (DZIF-8(In) sensor is 200 ℃ and CDs@DZIF-8(In) is 160 ℃) and 54% relative humidity was tested continuously, as shown in Fig. 5h. It can be observed that the Ra/Rg value of two sensors to 100 ppm TEA fluctuates slightly, but the fluctuation amplitude is very small, indicating that they have good long-term stability and a long service life. Selectivity is another an important characteristic of gas sensors. As shown in Fig. 5i, the response of CDs@DZIF-8(In) sensor to 11 types VOCs are all enhanced compared with the DZIF-8(In) sensor sensor. The Ra/Rg value of CDs@DZIF-8(In) sensor to 100 ppm VOCs at 160 ℃ is 374.6 for TEA, 98.1 for n-butanol, 76.7 for ammonia, 58 for ethanol, 49.6 for isopropanol, 21.4 for acetone, 27.1 for methanol, 26 for xylene, 18.3 for benzene, 19 for toluene, and 21.7 for methanal, respectively. Among them, the CDs@DZIF-8(In) sensor has the highest response to TEA, which was about 3.8, 4.9, 6.5, 7.6, 17.5, 13.8, 14.4, 20.4, 19.7 and 17.2 times higher than n-butanol, ammonia, ethanol, isopropanol, ethanediol, acetone, methanol, xylene, benzene, toluene, methanal, respectively. Furthermore, in multi-component systems, the selectivity of DZIF-8(In) and CDs@DZIF-8(In) to TEA has also been studied, as shown in Fig. S10(b). They still display good selectivity for TEA in the presence of ammonia and n-butyl. The Table 1 lists the gas sensing performance comparison between CDs@DZIF-8(In) gas sensing materials prepared in this work and ZnO/In2O3 gas sensing materials reported in other papers. Compared with other reported ZnO/In2O3 sensor, the porous hexagonal star CDs@DZIF-8(In) sensor shows high sensitivity (100 ppm), strong response (Rg/Ra = 374.6) and low operating temperature (160 ℃) for TEA. The charge transfer coefficients α was calculated from the Tafel plots before and after the presence of TEA (Fig. S13). The Tafel slopes of CDs@DZIF-8(In) and DZIF-8(In) were 2.37 and 2.21, respectively, while their Tafel slopes decreased to 1.55 and 1.90, respectively, after 100 ppm TEA addition. In Table S2, the charge transfer coefficient α can be calculated using the equation: [51].
b = 2.3RT/αF(2)
where R is the gas constant, T is the temperature, α is the electron transfer coefficient, and F is the Faraday constant.

The α of CDs@DZIF-8(In) is slightly smaller than that of DZIF-8(In), indicating that the charge transfer ability of the material itself decreases after compounding CDs and possesses a greater resistance. This is consistent with the experimental results that the initial resistance value of DZIF-8(In) increases after compounding CDs. And the α of both CDs@DZIF-8(In) and DZIF-8(In) increases after adding TEA, indicating that the charge transfer is accelerated after the presence of TEA, which is consistent with the decrease of the resistance of the material injected with TEA in the gas sensor. And the decreasing trend of CDs@DZIF-8(In) is larger and more responsive to TEA compared with DZIF-8(In), which demonstrates the superior sensing sensitivity of CDs@DZIF-8(In).

In the upper right of Fig. 6 , the CDs@DZIF-8(In) are composed of ZnO, In2O3 and CDs. Both ZnO and In2O3 are typical n-type semiconductors. The n-n heterostructures was form when they contact each other, which is conducive to efficient electron transmission. More importantly, when CDs are combined with DZIF-8(In), CDs@DZIF-8(In) response to TEA is greatly increased, and the operating temperature is significantly reduced. Compared with the original DZIF-8(In), CDs@DZIF-8(In) The excellent TEA sensing performance can be attributed to the electron transfer caused by the successful formation of heterojunction between DZIF-8(In) and CDs and the high activity of the edge of the CDs surface. [30–31] Firstly, the shielding effect caused by the introduction of CDs will reduce the effective nuclear charge. Therefore, the released electrons will be captured by oxygen molecules and further enhance the sensing performance. Secondly, as described in the XPS section, the percentage of Oads increase after the introduction of CDs. Due to the higher activity of Oads, the rich Oads crystal structure can provide more free electrons for the reaction between adsorbed oxygen and TEA gas, thus improving the sensing performance. Finally, the surface of the CDs has high chemical activity, which is conducive to the adsorption of TEA gas and oxygen molecules, so it can accelerate the reaction. Therefore, because of its unique physical and chemical properties, the introduction of CDs will significantly improve the sensing performance of the sensor.

The detailed sensing process is explained by the following chemical equations:
O2(gas) + e− →O2 −(ads)(3)
O2 −(ads) + e− →2O−(ads)(4)
(C2H5)3N → NH3 + 3C2H4(5)
NH3 + O− (ads)(or O2 −(ads)) → NO2 + H2O + e−(6)
C2H4 + O− (ads)(or O2 −(ads)) → CO2 + H2O + e−(7)

In short, there is the oxidation process of TEA:
(C2H5)3N + O− (ads)(or O2 −(ads)) → CO2 + NO2 + H2O + e−(8)

In the lower part of Fig. 6, because of the greater electronic affinity of oxygen molecules than the work function of semiconductors, when the CDs@DZIF-8(In) sensor is exposed to air, the O2 molecules would be adsorbed onto the surface of CDs@DZIF-8(In) and will withdraw thermally excited electrons from the conduction band to form more active chemisorbed oxygen species of O2 − and O−. Meanwhile, the resistance of CDs@DZIF-8(In) sensor will be increase due to the loss of electrons. In contrast, when the CDs@DZIF-8(In) sensor is exposed to TEA, the TEA molecules will be adsorbed on the surface of sensor and decomposed into NH3 and C2H4. The NH3 and C2H4 will be oxidized to NO2, CO2, and H2O by active chemisorbed oxygen species of O2 − and O−. Meanwhile, the chemical reaction will release free electrons back to the conduction band and decrease the sensor’s resistance.

In summary, as an excellent light-energy conversion module, the CDs can release more active electrons by forming chemical bonds with ZIF-8(In) and heterojunction with DZIF-8(In), thus improving greatly the detection performance of MOF and its derivatives for TEA. When the CDs were compounded with ZIF-8 (In), the CDs contribute to a stable and strong red fluorescence emission peak at 610 nm. The detection limit of CDs@ZIF-8(In) ratio type fluorescence sensor for TEA in water can reach to 1 ppm. When the CDs were compounded with DZIF-8 (In), the working temperature of CDs@DZIF-8(In) sensor can be reduced to 160 ℃, and the response value of ZIF-8 (In) to TEA is 3.5 times higher than that of the DZIF-8(In) sensor. The sensing platform sensitizing by CDs can realize the detection of TEA in both liquid and gas phase systems through the fluorescence detection and gas sensing. Our work provides a new idea for the design of a new high-performance gas sensing platform for TEA detection in multiphase systems, and can also be used as a general way to improve the performance of fluorescent sensors and gas sensors.
